jokes.text: You see, I'm a ninth-level octopriest in the Church of the Reasonableists. Oh, boy. The land is sacred in our religion because it's the place where all human souls will be transmuted when Zorp the Lizard God passes through Jupiter's Sphincter.
jokes.setup: Character presents himself as having religious evidence for land preservation
jokes.punchline: Reveals completely absurd cult belief system with Lizard God and Jupiter's Sphincter
jokes.explanation: Classic Parks & Rec absurdism with specific character types and memorable world-building. The escalating religious terminology is expertly crafted.

jokes.text: Hail Zorp.
jokes.setup: After the long explanation of the cult
jokes.punchline: Simple, matter-of-fact cult salute
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and timing. The casual delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: So, hold onto your straws, everybody, because Mama's going grasping.
jokes.setup: Leslie needs to find any reason to preserve the land
jokes.punchline: Clever play on 'grasping at straws' with physical metaphor
jokes.explanation: Smart wordplay that fits Leslie's character perfectly. Shows her self-awareness while maintaining determination.

jokes.text: In 1980, there were more than 600 million Indiana Brown Ants in that forest. Today, there are only 595 million.
jokes.punchline: Ridiculously small decrease in ant population over decades
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the payoff but not particularly memorable on its own.

jokes.text: At that rate, they'll all be gone by the year 203060.
jokes.punchline: Extinction projected 200,000 years in future
jokes.explanation: Perfect mathematical absurdity. The specific year makes it funnier than a vague 'distant future.'

jokes.text: Two hundred and three thousand and sixty? Two hundred thousand years from now? Yes. That doesn't seem like an immediate crisis.
jokes.punchline: Understated reaction to obviously non-urgent crisis
jokes.explanation: Solid straight-man reaction that grounds the absurdity.

jokes.text: An amusement park with rides and food and sexy cartoon characters. I call it Disneyland.
jokes.setup: Character proposes innovative amusement park concept
jokes.punchline: Claims to have invented Disneyland
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection but the joke structure is fairly predictable.

jokes.text: How did you get in here? Broke a window.
jokes.punchline: Matter-of-fact admission of breaking and entering
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and timing. Simple but effective.

jokes.text: You guys, I am Roscoe. I am Vice President of Cool New Shizz here at Gryzzl.
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous corporate title with deliberate misspelling
jokes.explanation: Good satire of tech company culture. Character-specific humor.

jokes.text: Leslie Knope has been meeting with people to try to sexy up her presentation. So, we had this far-out brain tornado.
jokes.punchline: Tech bro language with 'brain tornado' instead of brainstorm
jokes.explanation: Good character consistency with tech bro speak.

jokes.text: We should get a brity to come kick it with us small-time and then just be like, 'Hey, chums. Let's get tight with Gryzz.'
jokes.punchline: Mixture of slang terms and fake casual corporate speak
jokes.explanation: Excellent character voice. The awkward slang combinations feel authentic to the character type.

jokes.text: That's why I refuse to write my signature in cursive.
jokes.setup: Discussing preference for quality over flash
jokes.punchline: Bizarre example of anti-flash preference
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom Haverford logic - completely wrong understanding of sophistication. Very character-specific.

jokes.text: The Nets wouldn't be in Brooklyn if it weren't for Jay Z. And the sales for Chick-fil-A went through the roof once Elton John bought them.
jokes.punchline: Completely false claim about Elton John and Chick-fil-A
jokes.explanation: Classic Tom making up facts with complete confidence. The specificity makes it funnier.

jokes.text: And that's why we called on you home court chillers to frack your brainiums.
jokes.punchline: Tech bro speak taken to absurd extreme with 'frack your brainiums'
jokes.explanation: Escalation of the tech speak to ridiculous levels. 'Frack your brainiums' is memorable.

jokes.text: I got it from context.
jokes.punchline: Dry acknowledgment that he understood despite the nonsense
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ben Wyatt reaction. Economy and character consistency.

jokes.text: The only celebrities I recognize are furniture maker Garry Knox Bennett, designer of the notorious 16-penny nail cabinet, and Magnus, the 5x5 bull elk I saw near my cabin two years ago.
jokes.punchline: His 'celebrities' are an obscure furniture maker and a wild elk
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron Swanson. The specificity of the furniture maker and treating an elk as a celebrity is brilliant character work.

jokes.text: Took a shot at the bastard, and he stuck his antlers through the door of my meat shed. One day, Magnus, I will wear you as a jacket.
jokes.setup: Ron's encounter with the elk Magnus
jokes.punchline: Personal vendetta against an elk, threatening to wear it as clothing
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Ron. The personal rivalry with a wild animal and the specific threat is memorable and character-perfect.

jokes.text: You could be an airline pilot. I don't like heights. What about a submarine pilot? I don't like depths. I don't like anything, okay?
jokes.punchline: April's systematic rejection leading to nihilistic conclusion
jokes.explanation: Good escalation and character work. April's negativity is consistent and the progression is logical.

jokes.text: You say a word, I say a word, we see if it makes a profession. Executive. Trampoline. Computer. Trampoline.
jokes.setup: Word game to find career ideas
jokes.punchline: April says 'trampoline' to everything, defeating the purpose
jokes.explanation: April's passive resistance is in character. The repetition creates humor.

jokes.text: Well, I love it when you talk Point of Sale docs. You know it turns me on. But no.
jokes.punchline: Leslie's mock seduction followed by immediate rejection
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Leslie's playfulness despite her anger.

jokes.text: There's no way that I will be in the same room with Ron Swanson unless it is during a police lineup when I am pointing him out as the man who betrayed me.
jokes.punchline: Elaborate fantasy scenario of identifying Ron in a police lineup
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Leslie's dramatic tendencies. In character.

jokes.text: 'That's him, officer! Ronald Swanson.' 'Thank you, Leslie, that's the fifth crime you've solved this month. I'm not supposed to do this, but let me give you a badge and...'
jokes.punchline: Fantasy escalates to Leslie becoming honorary police detective
jokes.explanation: Classic Leslie fantasy escalation. Shows her need for recognition and justice.

jokes.text: I recently had a bit of a health scare, just a couple weeks in the hospital. Oh, God. This story's terrible so far.
jokes.punchline: Immediately acknowledges how depressing his own story sounds
jokes.explanation: Self-aware humor that's both funny and character-revealing. Good timing.

jokes.text: So, I fulfilled one of my lifelong dreams, and I became a Notary Public.
jokes.setup: Health scare motivating him to live life to the fullest
jokes.punchline: His dream is becoming a notary public
jokes.explanation: Good subversion of expectations. Anticlimactic dream is in character.

jokes.text: And infinity plus one year.
jokes.setup: Ben asking how long the feud will last after Leslie acts childish
jokes.punchline: Leslie doubles down with playground logic
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Ben's accusation. Shows Leslie's stubbornness.

jokes.text: That's nice, but I don't know if you can really consider me a celebrity. Yeah. That's your handwriting.
jokes.setup: Tom being modest about celebrity status
jokes.punchline: Someone points out he wrote his own name on the list
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom moment - fake modesty exposed. Good structure and character work.

jokes.text: That horrifying Lifestyle Newsletter filled with vegan recipes and pointless tchotchkes?
jokes.punchline: Ron's disgusted description of lifestyle content
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Ron's disdain for trendy lifestyle culture. 'Pointless tchotchkes' is a nice touch.

jokes.text: Bloosh has won the Pulitzer Prize for best top ten listicle two years in a row.
jokes.punchline: Pulitzer Prize for listicles (which doesn't exist)
jokes.explanation: Good satire of online media and fake prestige. Specific and believable.

jokes.text: I've recently self-published a biography about Harrison entitled 'Barely a President. William Henry Harrison's Thirty-Two Days in the White House.'
jokes.punchline: Title emphasizing how briefly Harrison was president
jokes.explanation: Clever title that captures both Harrison's insignificance and the historian's obsession.

jokes.text: Um, spoilers much? Now I'll never read that book.
jokes.setup: Historian reveals Harrison died after 32 days
jokes.punchline: Complaint about spoilers for historical events
jokes.explanation: Good meta joke about spoiler culture applied to historical facts.

jokes.text: That book sounded so boring, I cried a little. Oh, babe, I'm sorry you had to hear that. You're safe now.
jokes.punchline: April's dramatic reaction and Andy's protective response
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing April's dramatic tendencies and Andy's supportiveness.

jokes.text: Now, will you please consider investing in that toddler cologne I've been bugging you about? Toddler cologne. Baby, you smell good.
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous product idea and slogan
jokes.explanation: Peak Tom Haverford business idea. Absurd product with terrible slogan is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Terry, what's Leslie up to? She is trying to find a historical reason to earmark the land for preservation. Terry, come on, man
jokes.setup: Ben won't tell Ron about Leslie's plans
jokes.punchline: Ron immediately getting the info from Terry instead
jokes.explanation: Good setup/payoff showing the futility of Ben's discretion

jokes.text: Babe, we should sell our dumb house and buy this one.
jokes.setup: Looking at ruins of Harrison's lodge
jokes.punchline: Andy wants to buy a pile of old bricks
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy optimism and poor judgment. Visual contrast makes it funnier.

jokes.text: What? How old?
jokes.setup: Leslie mentions bag of old hamburgers as trash
jokes.punchline: Andy's immediate interest in potentially eating them
jokes.explanation: Classic Andy food obsession. Perfect economy and character consistency.

jokes.text: I found an artifact! Historical artifact! William Henry Harrison's wig. Andy. Put that down.
jokes.punchline: Andy grabs random trash thinking it's historical
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy setup with Andy's enthusiasm. Leslie's weary response is in character.

jokes.text: You're asking a notary to lie. I signed an oath, Ben. And then a different notary notarized the oath I signed.
jokes.setup: Ben suggesting they ignore missing page
jokes.punchline: Craig's extreme dedication to notary ethics with recursive oath
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of bureaucratic absurdity. The recursive notarization is a nice touch.

jokes.text: The If He'd Worn A Coat room explores how great America would have been if Harrison had worn a coat at his inauguration and not died.
jokes.punchline: Entire museum room dedicated to counterfactual coat-wearing
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist concept. The specificity of dedicating museum space to coat-wearing makes it memorable.

jokes.text: This room is called Other Things That Were Famous For One Month.
jokes.punchline: Room categorizing short-lived fame like Harrison's presidency
jokes.explanation: Good conceptual humor about fleeting fame. Connects well to the Harrison theme.

jokes.text: Oh, and side note, admission to this museum costs $14.
jokes.punchline: Expensive admission for worthless experience
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the museum's awfulness. The specific price point adds to the absurdity.

jokes.text: And side note, admission to this museum costs $14
jokes.punchline: The insult of charging significant money for this terrible museum
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation detail that makes the museum even more insulting

jokes.text: Why? Was there something inside the stupid ball? Ooh! Like a giant hamster?
jokes.setup: Discussion of Harrison campaign ball that drew crowds
jokes.punchline: Andy's excited suggestion about giant hamster
jokes.explanation: Good character interplay. Andy's enthusiasm for absurd explanation is in character.

jokes.text: This is exactly what I imagined heaven looks like. Right down to the unisex linen tunics.
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic appreciation for everything he would hate
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron sarcasm. His discomfort with lifestyle culture is well-established.

jokes.text: I was polishing my oyster forks with a cage-free olive oil rub.
jokes.punchline: Ridiculously precious lifestyle activity
jokes.explanation: Good parody of lifestyle culture excess. Specific details make it believable.

jokes.text: Apology accepted, and then I have no reaction to anything else you said.
jokes.punchline: Tom's honest confusion and dismissal
jokes.explanation: Great straight-man response. Tom's honesty about not understanding is refreshing.

jokes.text: Well, this season, I'm intrigued by asymmetrical overalls, angora toothbrushes, and locally sourced Italian flip-flops.
jokes.punchline: Series of nonsensical luxury items
jokes.explanation: Good parody list. Each item escalates the absurdity of lifestyle trends.

jokes.text: Now, try the hottest new craze, beef milk. It's like almond milk that's been squeezed through tiny holes in living cows.
jokes.punchline: Beef milk is literally just regular milk described pretentiously
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire. Takes lifestyle rebranding to its logical extreme by making milk sound exotic. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: That's milk. No. Milk costs $3 a gallon. Annabel's authentic, hand-strained teat-to-table Beef Milk? That costs $60 a gallon.
jokes.setup: Tom pointing out beef milk is just milk
jokes.punchline: Annabel's pricing strategy based on artisanal branding
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of the beef milk joke. The pricing disparity is absurd but believable for the character.

jokes.text: Yeah. And there's a wait list.
jokes.punchline: People are actually waiting to pay for overpriced milk
jokes.explanation: Perfect capstone to the beef milk sequence. Economy and escalation work well.

jokes.text: I just wanted to say that I really like your museum. It's weird and sad and unnecessary.
jokes.punchline: Compliment that's actually list of negatives she enjoys
jokes.explanation: Perfect April. Her appreciation for negative qualities is completely in character.

jokes.text: Though you do get a yearly stipend of 50,000 Harrison Bucks. Accepted only at that vending machine.
jokes.setup: Museum position is unpaid volunteer work
jokes.punchline: Worthless museum currency for single vending machine
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of museum's worthlessness. The specific currency name and limitation is funny.

jokes.text: The only things I like are dogs, sleeping late, and weird birthmarks.
jokes.punchline: Third item in list is unexpectedly specific and strange
jokes.explanation: Good character moment. The escalation to weird birthmarks is unexpected and fits April's character.

jokes.text: Zach Harrison? I went to camp with this guy. No one would ride in the boat with him because he had terrible BO. Then, he went with Janine, our counselor, and he fell asleep and got a boner.
jokes.setup: Introducing Harrison descendant as American royalty
jokes.punchline: Character deflates mystique with embarrassing camp stories
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Leslie's grand plans. The specific embarrassing details work well.

jokes.text: Notary publics can trace our origin all the way back to ancient Rome. No way. Calvin Coolidge was a notary.
jokes.punchline: Ben's fake enthusiasm for boring trivia
jokes.explanation: Good character interaction showing Ben's polite suffering through Craig's enthusiasm.

jokes.text: Playing with our dog, staring contests, sex with me, etc. And then, I lost the list. So, I had to make a new list. I didn't have any paper, so I wrote it on my leg.
jokes.setup: Andy making list of things April likes
jokes.punchline: Increasingly poor planning and execution
jokes.explanation: Good Andy logic progression. The escalating mishaps are in character.

jokes.text: They're one-on-one. You like dealing with people or dogs one-on-one.
jokes.setup: Andy analyzing what April's interests have in common
jokes.punchline: Lumping people and dogs together in the same category
jokes.explanation: Good Andy observation that accidentally reveals his perspective on social interaction.

jokes.text: Do you? No, that was just a hypothetical... Could that be possible? Because, you know, I've always thought that we shared very similar jawlines.
jokes.punchline: Zach immediately believes he might be related to Washington too
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Zach's gullibility and self-importance.

jokes.text: Zach! Camp Wamapoke. You got a boner. We'll catch up. What a dick.
jokes.setup: Zach entering press conference as dignity
jokes.punchline: Immediate embarrassment from past camp experience
jokes.explanation: Good callback and timing. Destroys Leslie's plans immediately.

jokes.text: Did he write letters to Thomas Jefferson on his rolltop desk, which, you know, I don't know, might have existed.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to sell the historical importance
jokes.punchline: Admits mid-speech that she's making up historical details
jokes.explanation: Great cringe moment where Leslie's desperation leads to transparent fabrication. The hesitant delivery sells it.

jokes.text: Uh... Harrison's a pretty common last name.
jokes.setup: Leslie asking about being Harrison descendant
jokes.punchline: Zach deflates the significance of his connection
jokes.explanation: Good deflation moment. Zach's honesty undermines Leslie's presentation.

jokes.text: 'Wow, that was cool', said some boring idiot from 200 years ago.
jokes.setup: Historical reenactment of campaign ball rolling
jokes.punchline: Narrator acknowledging how lame the whole thing was
jokes.explanation: Good meta humor that acknowledges the absurdity of historical enthusiasm.

jokes.text: Here to introduce our vision is Annabel Porter from Bloosh, and the sexy ladies known in Pawnee as the Somebody's Daughter dancers.
jokes.punchline: The awkward name 'Somebody's Daughter dancers' for what's clearly meant to be sexy entertainment
jokes.explanation: Brilliant name that captures small-town awkwardness about sexy entertainment. The name acknowledges that these are someone's daughters.

jokes.text: Fresh. Innovative. Placemaking. Disposable duvets. Growth hacking. Super Moon. Gryzzl.
jokes.punchline: Nonsensical corporate buzzwords with random luxury items
jokes.explanation: Good satire of corporate presentation style. The random buzzwords work well.

jokes.text: Because I've died somehow and now I'm a ghost living in purgatory until I complete my unfinished business.
jokes.setup: Ben's frustration with the document signing process
jokes.punchline: Elaborate metaphysical explanation for bureaucratic hell
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Ben's frustration into existential territory. Character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Ron. Ron. That's not even a word.
jokes.setup: Leslie listing everything she hates about Ron
jokes.punchline: Attacking his name as not being a real word
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of anger to absurd levels. Shows Leslie's tendency to over-argue.

jokes.text: You're not that good at scrapbooking.
jokes.setup: Heated argument between Leslie and Ron
jokes.punchline: Ron's devastating personal attack about Leslie's hobby
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment. Ron's idea of a cruel personal attack is critique of scrapbooking skills. Perfect economy and character understanding.

jokes.text: That feud is, like, Biggie-Tupac level. Maybe even Morgan Freeman-Shailene Woodley level. Naw. It's bad, but it's not that bad.
jokes.setup: Comparing Leslie-Ron feud to famous feuds
jokes.punchline: Made-up Freeman-Woodley feud treated as worse than Biggie-Tupac
jokes.explanation: Good escalation and misdirection. The random pairing and escalation works well.

jokes.text: I'll let you rename the triplets Ruth, Bader, and Ginsburg.
jokes.setup: Ben trying to convince Leslie to cooperate
jokes.punchline: Offering to name babies after Supreme Court justice
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Ben knows exactly what Leslie wants. The RBG reference is perfect for Leslie.

jokes.text: This has gone too far, and you are not leaving this office until you've made peace.
jokes.setup: Ben locking Leslie and Ron in the office
jokes.punchline: Ben taking extreme parental action with two adults
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the ending. Ben's parent-like intervention is appropriate for the childish behavior.
